title: Opioids on spinal neurons: increased K+ efflux (hyperpolarization)
vignette: A 44-year-old woman with a history of breast cancer comes to the office due to persistent back pain. The patient was recently started on radiation treatment for several vertebral metastases. She has taken acetaminophen and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs but continues to have severe pain. On physical examination, she has tenderness over several lumbar vertebrae. Neurologic examination is unremarkable. The patient is started on oral morphine therapy. Which of the following is the most likely direct effect of this medication on the spinal cord neurons of this patient?

clues.note: Bony metastatic cancer pain is severe and often needs opioids when non-opioids fail. This sets up morphine and a question about its cellular mechanism in the spinal cord (dorsal horn) pain pathway.
Failure of acetaminophen/NSAIDs justifies escalating to an opioid for cancer pain. The clinical reasoning is just to get to morphine; the tested point is its ion-channel effect on neurons.

Morphine is a μ-opioid receptor agonist (Gi-coupled). On POSTSYNAPTIC neurons it opens potassium channels → increased K+ EFFLUX → hyperpolarization (harder to fire). Presynaptically it closes voltage-gated Ca2+ channels → less neurotransmitter release. The question asks the direct neuronal effect → increased K+ efflux.

whatMatters: The cancer-pain story just leads to morphine. The tested mechanism: μ-opioid receptors are Gi-coupled; on spinal cord neurons they OPEN potassium channels, increasing K+ EFFLUX and hyperpolarizing the neuron so it's less excitable. Answer E. (Presynaptically they also close Ca2+ channels to reduce neurotransmitter release.)
strategy: For opioid cellular mechanism, remember two coupled actions of Gi-linked μ receptors: postsynaptic → ↑K+ efflux → hyperpolarization; presynaptic → ↓Ca2+ influx → ↓neurotransmitter (substance P/glutamate) release. Both decrease pain transmission. If asked the effect that hyperpolarizes the neuron, pick increased K+ efflux.

options.text: Increased calcium influx into the cells
Increased chloride influx into the cells
Increased potassium efflux out of the cells

eliminations.label: Na/Ca exchange — not the opioid mechanism
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the Na/Ca exchanger moves calcium across membranes (e.g., in cardiac myocytes). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: opioids act through Gi-coupled receptors on K+ and Ca2+ channels, not by activating Na/Ca exchange in spinal neurons. ✅ Correct if: the topic were cardiac calcium handling/digoxin physiology.

eliminations.label: block Na+ influx — that's local anesthetics
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: blocking voltage-gated sodium channels prevents action-potential propagation — the mechanism of LOCAL ANESTHETICS (lidocaine). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: morphine doesn't block Na+ channels; it hyperpolarizes via K+ efflux and reduces Ca2+-dependent transmitter release. ✅ Correct if: the drug were a local anesthetic or a Na-channel-blocking antiarrhythmic.

eliminations.label: increased Ca2+ influx — backwards (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: increased calcium influx would PROMOTE neurotransmitter release and excitability. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: opioids do the opposite — they CLOSE presynaptic Ca2+ channels to REDUCE transmitter release; they don't increase Ca2+ influx. ✅ Correct if: the question described an excitatory mechanism increasing transmitter release (the opposite of analgesia).

eliminations.label: increased Cl- influx — that's GABA-A/benzodiazepines
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: increased chloride influx hyperpolarizes neurons via GABA-A receptors (benzodiazepines, barbiturates) or glycine receptors. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: that's a different receptor system; opioids hyperpolarize through K+ efflux, not Cl- influx. ✅ Correct if: the drug were a benzodiazepine/barbiturate acting on GABA-A chloride channels.

reasoning: Cancer pain unresponsive to non-opioids → morphine
Postsynaptic effect: open K+ channels → increased K+ efflux → hyperpolarization
Direct neuronal effect = increased potassium efflux → answer E
mechanism: Opioids bind Gi-protein-coupled μ (and κ/δ) receptors. The βγ subunits OPEN inwardly rectifying potassium channels, increasing K+ EFFLUX and hyperpolarizing the POSTSYNAPTIC neuron, while Gi inhibition closes presynaptic voltage-gated Ca2+ channels to reduce release of pain neurotransmitters (substance P, glutamate). Decreased cAMP also contributes. Net effect in the spinal dorsal horn: reduced transmission of nociceptive signals → analgesia.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Pharmacology > Opioids: μ/κ/δ receptors, Gi-coupled — ↑K+ efflux (postsynaptic hyperpolarization) + ↓Ca2+ influx (↓ presynaptic transmitter release) + ↓cAMP; sites (periphery, dorsal horn, brain). Cross-link the opiate-analgesia diagram.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Map the channel effect to 'less firing': hyperpolarization needs either K+ OUT or Cl- IN. Opioids use K+ efflux. The other listed effect (↓Ca2+ influx) is presynaptic; the asked 'direct effect' here is the K+ one.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on Gi-coupled receptor, hyperpolarization, K+ efflux, voltage-gated Ca2+ channel, substance P, presynaptic vs postsynaptic? Tell me which and I'll explain.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Increased Ca2+ influx (C, your pick) is the OPPOSITE of what opioids do — they close Ca2+ channels. And Cl- influx (D) is the GABA/benzo mechanism. Opioid hyperpolarization = K+ efflux.

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: The opiate-analgesia figure shows the μ receptor closing presynaptic Ca2+ channels (↓ neurotransmitter release) and opening postsynaptic K+ channels (efflux → hyperpolarization). Practice reading the pre- vs postsynaptic actions.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Missed as C → memorize the two opioid actions: postsynaptic K+ OUT (hyperpolarize) + presynaptic Ca2+ channel CLOSED (less transmitter). Both reduce pain signaling.

keywords.front: Net opioid effect in dorsal horn

firstAid.detail: Pharmacology > Analgesics: μ/κ/δ Gi receptors, ↑K+ efflux + ↓Ca2+ influx + ↓cAMP, analgesia, tolerance/dependence, naloxone.
firstAid.location: Pharmacology > Analgesics: μ/κ/δ Gi receptors, ↑K+ efflux + ↓Ca2+ influx + ↓cAMP, analgesia, tolerance/dependence, naloxone.

firstAid.detail: Pharm: local anesthetics (Na+ block), benzodiazepines/barbiturates (Cl- influx via GABA-A), Ca2+ channel roles in transmitter release.
firstAid.location: Pharm: local anesthetics (Na+ block), benzodiazepines/barbiturates (Cl- influx via GABA-A), Ca2+ channel roles in transmitter release.

firstAid.detail: Pharm/IM: WHO analgesic ladder, opioids for metastatic bone pain, adjuvants.
firstAid.location: Pharm/IM: WHO analgesic ladder, opioids for metastatic bone pain, adjuvants.

memoryHook: Opioids 'calm' the neuron: K+ OUT (hyperpolarize) + Ca2+ channels CLOSED (less transmitter). Increased K+ efflux = the off switch.
